longTailKeywords: frozen shoulder how long does it last

h1Title: Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Frozen shoulder, clinically termed adhesive capsulitis, is a progressive fibro-contractile condition of the glenohumeral joint capsule that restricts shoulder movement in all planes, typically passing through three recognizable phases: freezing (painful stiffness), frozen (restricted but less painful), and thawing (gradual recovery). It affects 2–5% of the general population and up to 20% of people with diabetes mellitus. Women and adults aged 40–60 account for the majority of cases. Without intervention the condition resolves in 18 months to 3 years in most patients, but early treatment with corticosteroid injections and physical therapy accelerates recovery substantially. A minority of cases require hydrodilatation or manipulation under anaesthesia.

keyStats.prevalence: 2–5% of the general population; up to 19–20% lifetime risk in people with type 1 or type 2 diabetes mellitus
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 1.4–2× more often than men; peak age 40–60 years; bilateral in 6–17% of idiopathic cases
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset age 45–60; mean age at presentation 55 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 14–20 million people affected globally at any time; 200,000–400,000 new diagnoses annually in the US

definition: Adhesive capsulitis (ICD-10: M75.0) is a fibro-inflammatory condition of the glenohumeral joint characterized by progressive capsular contraction and adhesion formation that restrict active and passive range of motion in all planes, particularly external rotation. The condition was first described by Duplay in 1872 and named 'adhesive capsulitis' by Neviaser in 1945 following operative findings of capsular adhesion to the humeral head. Histopathology shows fibroblastic proliferation, myofibroblast transformation, and type III collagen deposition in the capsule and rotator interval, with relative absence of cartilage damage. The axillary fold of the capsule, which normally distends to allow 180° abduction, becomes contracted and thickened to 3–4× normal thickness. The rotator interval — the triangular space between the subscapularis and supraspinatus tendons bridged by the coracohumeral ligament — is universally contracted in frozen shoulder and is a primary target of both injectable and surgical treatment. Physical medicine and rehabilitation specialists, rheumatologists, and shoulder surgeons manage the condition; the majority of cases are treated non-operatively.

typesClassification.description: No identifiable precipitant; associated with diabetes, thyroid disease, and autoimmune conditions. Accounts for 70–80% of cases. Has the classic three-phase course of 18–30 months average duration.

Arises after a defined shoulder event: rotator cuff tear (immobility-related), shoulder surgery, prolonged immobilization after fracture or stroke, or external trauma. Capsular contracture follows the underlying injury. Treatment targets both the capsular contracture and the precipitating condition.

Occurs in 20% of people with diabetes; tends to be more severe, bilateral, and resistant to treatment than idiopathic cases. Advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) accumulate in collagen, promoting cross-linking and fibro-contracture.

primarySymptoms: Progressive pain in the outer shoulder and upper arm that starts insidiously over weeks, often worse at night and interrupting sleep — hallmark of the freezing phase, lasting 2–9 months.
Loss of external rotation range of motion, typically the earliest and most pronounced directional loss: patients cannot reach behind the back to fasten a bra or tuck in a shirt.
Loss of abduction — reaching overhead or to the side becomes progressively restricted; the patient compensates by shrugging the shoulder (ipsilateral trunk shift).
Reduced internal rotation — difficulty reaching up the back; the hand can no longer reach the opposite scapula.
Pain that begins to subside in the frozen phase (months 4–12 typical) even as stiffness remains maximal, producing the characteristic dissociation between pain and motion restriction.
Gradual spontaneous recovery of motion in the thawing phase (months 12–36), starting with external rotation, then abduction, then internal rotation.
Referred pain along the outer arm and deltoid insertion, sometimes reaching the elbow but rarely the hand — helps distinguish from cervical radiculopathy.
Capsular end-feel on passive examination — an abrupt, non-yielding restriction in external rotation and abduction that distinguishes frozen shoulder from rotator cuff tears.
Bilateral symptoms in 6–17% of cases, rarely simultaneously — the second shoulder typically develops months to years after the first.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained nocturnal shoulder aching that prevents lying on the affected side — often the first symptom before motion loss is apparent.
Painful arc on abduction with a gradual (not sudden) onset in a diabetic or post-menopausal woman aged 40–60, particularly if the shoulder was never previously injured.
Loss of 10–20° external rotation compared with the contralateral side noted incidentally during a routine musculoskeletal examination.
emergencySigns: Severe acute-onset shoulder pain after minor trauma with inability to move the shoulder at all — should exclude acute rotator cuff tear, shoulder dislocation, or fracture before attributing to frozen shoulder.
Fever, systemic illness, or erythema overlying the shoulder with acute pain — septic arthritis requires urgent aspiration and antibiotics.
Progressive neurological deficit in the arm (weakness, wasting, sensory loss) — Parsonage-Turner syndrome or cervical myelopathy must be excluded urgently.

causes.description: In primary frozen shoulder no precipitant is identified. Inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, TGF-β) upregulate fibroblast and myofibroblast proliferation in the joint capsule and rotator interval, leading to progressive collagen deposition and loss of capsular volume. The trigger for initial cytokine release is unknown.

Advanced glycation end-products accumulate in glenohumeral capsular collagen in hyperglycemic states, promoting cross-linking and reduced capsular extensibility. Diabetic patients develop frozen shoulder at 4–5× the rate of non-diabetic controls and respond less well to corticosteroid injection.

causes.cause: Shoulder immobilization after injury or surgery
causes.description: Prolonged rest after rotator cuff tear, shoulder surgery, fracture, or axillary surgery (e.g., mastectomy) reduces joint lubrication, promotes synovial adhesion, and triggers capsular contracture. Early post-operative mobilization reduces secondary frozen shoulder incidence by approximately 60%.

Hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism both increase frozen shoulder risk 2–3×. Thyroid disease affects collagen metabolism and promotes periarticular fibrosis. Treating the thyroid disorder does not always resolve the shoulder contracture.

Dupuytren contracture is present in 15–20% of frozen shoulder patients, suggesting shared myofibroblast-driven fibroproliferative biology. Autoimmune thyroiditis and systemic lupus erythematosus are also over-represented in cohort studies.

riskFactors.description: Strongest modifiable risk factor: lifetime incidence 19–20% in type 1 and 2 diabetes; bilateral in 40–50% of diabetic cases. Poor glycemic control accelerates progression and reduces treatment response.
Women represent 55–65% of frozen shoulder patients. Hormonal shifts at perimenopause may alter fibroblast activity; postmenopausal women on no hormone therapy show higher rates.
Risk increases sharply after age 40; rare before 30. Cumulative collagen changes and reduced synovial fluid production with ageing lower the capsule's threshold for contracture.

riskFactors.factor: Shoulder immobility after injury or surgery
riskFactors.description: Any cause of shoulder immobility — sling use after fracture, post-mastectomy recovery, prolonged sling after rotator cuff repair — markedly increases secondary frozen shoulder risk.
Thyroid dysfunction increases frozen shoulder risk 2–3×. Subclinical hypothyroidism detected on screening is present in up to 20% of frozen shoulder patients referred to tertiary clinics.
Dupuytren disease is found in 15–20% of frozen shoulder patients, reflecting a shared myofibroblast fibroproliferative diathesis. Presence of one condition should prompt screening for the other.
Up to 17% of patients develop a second frozen shoulder in the contralateral shoulder, usually within 5 years of the first.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 40–60: account for approximately 60% of all frozen shoulder diagnoses; highest incidence is in perimenopausal and early postmenopausal women.
People with diabetes: lifetime incidence of 19–20%; more severe, more bilateral, and more treatment-resistant than non-diabetic cases.
Post-operative shoulder patients: 1–5% of patients immobilized after shoulder surgery or axillary procedures develop secondary adhesive capsulitis.

People with stroke or neurological upper-limb paresis: shoulder-hand syndrome and frozen shoulder are common complications of hemiplegia, occurring in up to 30% of stroke survivors with arm involvement.
diagnosisOverview: Frozen shoulder is diagnosed clinically on the basis of progressive, global restriction of glenohumeral range of motion — both active and passive — in all planes, with no plain-radiograph evidence of glenohumeral arthritis or fracture. The examiner documents external rotation (ER), abduction, and internal rotation in both arms; loss of ER of more than 50% compared with the contralateral shoulder, combined with a capsular end-feel, is highly characteristic. Standard clinical criteria require loss of ER to fewer than 50% of normal, abduction below 100°, and symptoms persisting more than 3 months without a structural cause on imaging. Plain radiographs are taken to exclude glenohumeral osteoarthritis, calcific tendinitis, and acromioclavicular joint disease. MRI with gadolinium shows capsular thickening above 4 mm at the rotator interval and axillary recess, and is used when rotator cuff tear is suspected as a concurrent or primary diagnosis. Ultrasound identifies rotator interval thickening and increased vascularity but has lower diagnostic accuracy than MRI for capsular disease. Arthrography — either conventional or as part of hydrodilatation — confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating reduced glenohumeral capsular volume (under 10 mL versus the normal 20–35 mL). Distinguishing frozen shoulder from rotator cuff tear requires careful examination: both restrict active abduction, but rotator cuff tear shows passive abduction preserved with a specific empty-arc impingement sign or a lag sign, while frozen shoulder restricts passive motion equally with a capsular end-feel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies restriction in external rotation, abduction, and internal rotation; tracks progress over time and guides treatment decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician uses a handheld goniometer and moves the arm through its range. Takes 5–10 minutes. Findings are compared with the unaffected shoulder. No discomfort beyond normal stiffness.

diagnosticTests.test: Shoulder plain radiographs (AP, lateral, axillary views)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes glenohumeral osteoarthritis, calcific tendinitis, fracture, and acromioclavicular joint disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to three X-ray positions; the axillary view requires gentle arm elevation. Results available immediately. No contrast or preparation required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates capsular thickness at the rotator interval and axillary recess, identifies concurrent rotator cuff tears, labral pathology, and rules out occult fracture or bone tumour
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–45 minutes lying still. A gadolinium injection may be given to highlight capsular signal. No special preparation beyond removing metal objects.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses rotator cuff integrity, identifies calcific deposits, and measures rotator interval thickness; useful for guiding injections
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld transducer scans the shoulder in multiple positions. Takes 15–20 minutes. Dynamic examination includes arm rotation. Painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates reduced capsular volume (below 10 mL) confirming adhesive capsulitis; also performed as the first step of hydrodilatation treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast dye is injected into the glenohumeral joint under fluoroscopic guidance. Mild pressure sensation during injection. Result immediately available; procedure also acts as initial treatment via hydrodilatation.

treatmentOverview: Management of frozen shoulder follows a phase-specific, step-up protocol. British Elbow and Shoulder Society (BESS) and AAOS guidance align on corticosteroid injection as the most effective early intervention: one to three glenohumeral injections of triamcinolone 40 mg (or methylprednisolone 80 mg) combined with 5 mL bupivacaine, administered under ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance, reduce pain and improve function more rapidly than physiotherapy or NSAID alone, especially in the freezing phase. Early physiotherapy is added after injection to capitalise on pain control — passive pendular exercises, external rotation stretching, and joint mobilization should begin within 2 weeks of injection. Repeated corticosteroid injections are limited to three over 12 months due to cartilage toxicity concerns; a new injection is typically not given less than 6 weeks after the preceding one. Hydrodilatation — controlled high-volume distension of the glenohumeral capsule with saline, corticosteroid, and local anaesthetic under fluoroscopy — accelerates recovery more than injection alone in several randomized trials, particularly in the frozen phase. A 2020 Cochrane review found hydrodilatation produced clinically meaningful improvements in pain and function at 6 weeks compared with corticosteroid injection alone. For patients who have not recovered after 6 months of conservative treatment, manipulation under anaesthesia (MUA) or arthroscopic capsular release are definitive surgical options. MUA tears capsular adhesions under sedation and achieves 80–90% good or excellent functional recovery; arthroscopic release is preferred in diabetic patients and when MUA fails, as it allows selective rotator-interval and inferior capsule division under direct vision with success rates of 85–95%.

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into the glenohumeral joint — ideally under ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance into the posterior recess. Rapidly reduces capsular inflammation and night pain. Most effective in the freezing phase; less effective once dense fibrosis predominates in the frozen phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 40–60% at 6 weeks vs. 20–30% for physiotherapy alone; effect maintained at 3 months in 60–70% of patients (Buchbinder et al., Cochrane 2003)

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrodilatation (capsular distension arthrography)
medicalTreatments.description: Fluoroscopy-guided injection of up to 30–40 mL of saline, corticosteroid, and local anaesthetic into the glenohumeral joint, forcibly distending the contracted capsule. The volume tears the inferior and anterior capsular adhesions. Procedure takes 15 minutes as an outpatient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2020 review: significant improvement in pain and function vs. corticosteroid injection alone at 6 weeks; NNT approximately 3 for clinically meaningful response

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily or ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily, 4–6 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course oral NSAIDs are useful for nocturnal pain in the freezing phase as a bridge to injection. Standalone efficacy is limited for the underlying capsular contracture.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mild to moderate reduction in VAS pain score; no evidence of effect on ROM recovery as a standalone treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Supervised physical therapy (pendular mobilization, passive stretching, joint mobilization)
medicalTreatments.description: Best initiated 1–2 weeks after corticosteroid injection when pain is reduced. Programmes include pendular exercises, cross-body stretch, sleeper stretch, external rotation stretch, and progressive glenohumeral joint mobilization (Maitland Grade III–IV).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Alone produces 20–30% improvement in ROM at 3 months; combined with injection achieves 50–70% ROM restoration at 6 months

medicalTreatments.name: Suprascapular nerve block
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of local anaesthetic (± corticosteroid) around the suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch. Provides analgesia for 3–6 weeks to facilitate physiotherapy when intra-articular injection is contraindicated or insufficient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable pain reduction to intra-articular injection at 12 weeks in two RCTs; allows physiotherapy participation in refractory pain cases

surgicalOptions.name: Manipulation under anaesthesia (MUA)
surgicalOptions.description: The shoulder is forcibly moved through full range under general anaesthesia or brachial plexus block, tearing capsular adhesions. Full ROM is typically achieved on the table. Physiotherapy begins within 24–48 hours to prevent re-adhesion.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good or excellent outcome at 1 year in 80–90%; refractory cases may require repeat or conversion to arthroscopic release

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic capsular release
surgicalOptions.description: Arthroscope introduced via posterior portal; the rotator interval, anterior, and inferior capsule are divided electrosurgically under direct vision. Preserves subscapularis. Preferred for diabetic patients, failed MUA, and bilateral disease. General or regional anaesthesia; day-surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85–95% good or excellent outcome at 12 months; superior to MUA for diabetic cases in comparative studies

alternativeTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT)
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2021 meta-analysis of 5 RCTs found focused ESWT produced statistically significant improvements in shoulder ROM and pain scores at 3 months compared with sham treatment, with an effect size comparable to corticosteroid injection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture (adjunct analgesia)
alternativeTreatments.description: A Cochrane-registered systematic review (Ma et al. 2018, Evid Based Complement Altern Med) found that acupuncture as an adjunct to physiotherapy improved shoulder function scores by 15–25% more than physiotherapy alone at 6 weeks, though evidence quality was low.

whySeeSpecialist: A physiatrist, rheumatologist, or shoulder surgeon should assess frozen shoulder when symptoms last more than 6 weeks, when a concurrent rotator cuff tear is suspected, or when there is no response to two corticosteroid injections at 6-week intervals. Surgical planning for MUA or arthroscopic release requires a shoulder surgeon.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a musculoskeletal specialist or shoulder surgeon who performs ultrasound-guided injections and has experience with hydrodilatation, as injection accuracy significantly affects outcomes. For surgery, select a surgeon who performs at least 30–50 arthroscopic capsular releases per year.
hospitalCriteria: Fluoroscopy or ultrasound capability for guided injection and hydrodilatation
Physiotherapy department experienced in shoulder mobilization post-injection
Anaesthetic support for manipulation under anaesthesia (MUA)
Arthroscopic shoulder surgical suite for capsular release
Endocrinology support for concurrent management of diabetes
keyFacilities: Day-surgery centers with arthroscopic shoulder capability
Musculoskeletal radiology services for fluoroscopic hydrodilatation
Diabetic shoulder clinics in tertiary referral centers
preventionStrategies: Begin early gentle pendular shoulder exercises within 48–72 hours of any shoulder immobilization (post-fracture, post-surgery, sling use) — the most evidence-supported preventive measure.
Optimize glycemic control in diabetes mellitus; HbA1c below 7% is associated with lower frozen shoulder incidence and better injection response.
Screen for and treat thyroid dysfunction in patients presenting with early unexplained shoulder stiffness.
Avoid prolonged sling use after minor shoulder injuries — the sling should be discontinued and gentle ROM resumed as soon as pain allows, guided by a physiotherapist.
After stroke or brachial plexus injury, initiate passive shoulder mobilization programs within the first 2 weeks to prevent secondary adhesive capsulitis.
lifestyleModifications: Perform daily home shoulder stretches (cross-body stretch, external-rotation door-frame stretch, pendular exercises) for at least 20 minutes, as prescribed by a physiotherapist.
Apply moist heat to the shoulder for 10–15 minutes before stretching to increase capsular extensibility.
Sleep on the unaffected shoulder or supine with a pillow supporting the arm in 30° abduction to reduce nocturnal pain.
Modify overhead and behind-back activities to stay within the pain-free range during recovery, avoiding aggressive forced stretching that provokes capsular flare.
Maintain cardiovascular fitness with walking or stationary cycling — lower-limb exercise preserves fitness without loading the shoulder.
Attend all physiotherapy sessions; compliance with home exercise programs is the strongest predictor of successful non-surgical recovery.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) to reduce systemic inflammatory tone
Low glycemic index carbohydrates to support optimal glycemic control in diabetic patients
Adequate protein (1.2 g/kg/day) and vitamin D (800–1000 IU daily if deficient) to support connective tissue remodelling
Fruits and vegetables high in antioxidants (berries, leafy greens, citrus) to limit oxidative stress on periarticular tissues
avoid: High-glycemic foods and added sugars — worsen glycemic control and accelerate collagen cross-linking in diabetic frozen shoulder
Excessive alcohol consumption — impairs tissue healing and collagen metabolism
High-sodium ultra-processed foods associated with systemic inflammation

exerciseGuidelines: During the freezing phase, gentle pendular (Codman) exercises within comfort range are indicated. Avoid forceful aggressive stretching during peak inflammation — it provokes flares and delays recovery. As pain subsides in the frozen phase, progress to passive and active-assisted ROM exercises, external-rotation stretching, and later resistance exercises for scapular stabilizers and rotator cuff. Full weight-bearing shoulder exercise resumes only after 90% ROM recovery, typically at 18–30 months from onset or 6–12 weeks after surgical release.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed home shoulder exercises for 20–30 minutes each morning after a warm shower or moist heat application.
Use prescribed pain medication or NSAID before sleep to manage nocturnal pain during the freezing phase.
Modify daily activities (dressing, driving, overhead reach) to avoid exceeding the pain-free range; use a long-handled dressing stick or adaptive tools if needed.
Track ROM at home by measuring how far the arm can reach laterally and behind the back — improvement should be visible over 4-week intervals.
Attend physiotherapy twice weekly during active treatment phases; frequency can reduce to once weekly during the thawing phase.
Monitor blood glucose tightly if diabetic; report any worsening of shoulder stiffness to the treating team as it may indicate suboptimal glycemic control.
prognosis: Most patients with idiopathic frozen shoulder recover to near-normal function without surgery, though the natural history spans 18 months to 3 years. Studies with 5–10 year follow-up show that 90% of patients regain full or near-full shoulder function, while 10–15% report persistent mild restriction or pain. Early intervention with corticosteroid injection shortens the freezing phase by 4–6 weeks and improves function at 6 months. Hydrodilatation produces faster pain and ROM recovery than injection alone in the frozen phase. Surgical outcomes (MUA, arthroscopic release) are excellent: 85–95% good or excellent result at 12 months. Negative prognostic factors include diabetes mellitus (slower recovery, higher recurrence), bilateral disease, duration above 12 months before treatment, and poor baseline ROM. Postoperative physiotherapy compliance is the strongest predictor of maintained surgical gains.
recoveryTimeline: Natural history: freezing phase 2–9 months, frozen phase 4–12 months, thawing 6–24 months — total 18–36 months. With corticosteroid injection + physiotherapy: significant improvement within 6–12 weeks, functional recovery at 3–6 months. Post-hydrodilatation: improvement by 4–8 weeks. Post-MUA or arthroscopic release: return to most daily activities within 4–8 weeks, full recovery by 3–6 months.
complications: Persistent motion restriction in 10–15% of patients despite full treatment, particularly in long-standing diabetic cases with dense fibrosis.
Rotator cuff tear — occurs in approximately 1–2% of MUA cases, especially in osteoporotic patients with very stiff shoulders; arthroscopic release reduces this risk.

Glenohumeral chondral damage from repeated high-dose intra-articular corticosteroid injections (more than four injections per shoulder per year); risk minimized by limiting total injections to three per 12-month period.
Axillary nerve injury (rare) during MUA or arthroscopic release; presents as deltoid weakness postoperatively, usually resolves within 3 months.
Secondary adhesive capsulitis in the contralateral shoulder — occurs in up to 17% of patients within 5 years; early physiotherapy education reduces risk.
Bilateral frozen shoulder in diabetic patients — occurs in 40–50% of diabetic cases and requires coordinated bilateral management.

supportResources.name: BESS (British Elbow and Shoulder Society) — Patient Frozen Shoulder Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative UK specialist society guide on phases, treatment options, and expected recovery.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — Frozen Shoulder Overview
supportResources.description: AAOS evidence-based patient information on adhesive capsulitis diagnosis and treatment ladder.

supportResources.name: Versus Arthritis — Frozen Shoulder Guide
supportResources.description: UK patient organization with clear explanation of phases, exercises, and specialist referral guidance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy / Partial Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rotator cuff tendinopathy restricts active abduction but preserves passive ROM with a smooth, non-capsular end-feel and a painful arc at 60–120°. Frozen shoulder restricts both active AND passive motion equally in all planes with a firm capsular end-feel, especially in external rotation. MRI or ultrasound confirms rotator cuff integrity.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glenohumeral Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis produces pain and restricted motion with osteophytes and joint-space narrowing on plain radiograph. Frozen shoulder shows normal radiographic joint space. Both restrict external rotation, but OA has a bony end-feel and crepitus rather than the fibrous capsular end-feel of adhesive capsulitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervical Radiculopathy (C5–C6)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: C5 radiculopathy refers pain to the outer shoulder and upper arm with weakness of deltoid or biceps and a diminished biceps reflex. Shoulder range of motion is preserved unless disuse supervenes. Frozen shoulder shows global ROM restriction with preserved reflexes. Cervical MRI and nerve conduction studies differentiate the two.

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently co-exists with or precedes frozen shoulder; rotator cuff pain leading to shoulder disuse is a recognised precipitant of secondary adhesive capsulitis.

relatedConditions.name: De Quervain Tenosynovitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are stenosing fibro-contractile tendon conditions managed by physical medicine specialists; postpartum women are at elevated risk for both simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Post-Stroke Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary frozen shoulder is one of the commonest painful complications of stroke-related upper-limb paresis and is managed within stroke rehabilitation programs.

relatedConditions.name: Myofascial Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Shoulder and periscapular trigger points frequently accompany frozen shoulder and are addressed in the same rehabilitation program to maximise range-of-motion recovery.

faqs.question: What is frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis)?
faqs.answer: Frozen shoulder is a condition where the glenohumeral joint capsule becomes inflamed and then progressively contracts and scars, restricting movement in all directions. It passes through three phases: painful freezing, stiff frozen, and slowly recovering thawing. It affects 2–5% of the population and is especially common in people with diabetes.

faqs.question: What are the three phases of frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: The freezing phase (2–9 months) brings increasing pain and early stiffness. The frozen phase (4–12 months) is characterised by maximal stiffness with less pain. The thawing phase (6–24 months) brings gradual spontaneous recovery of motion. Total duration averages 18–30 months but varies widely.

faqs.question: What causes frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are diabetes mellitus (20% of people with diabetes develop it), shoulder immobility after injury or surgery, thyroid disease, and idiopathic fibroblastic capsular inflammation. Dupuytren contracture is associated in 15–20% of cases. In many patients no precipitant is identified.

faqs.question: How is frozen shoulder diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: global restriction of both active and passive shoulder movement with a firm capsular end-feel, especially in external rotation. X-rays exclude arthritis and fracture. MRI shows rotator interval and capsular thickening. Arthrography (reduced joint volume below 10 mL) confirms the diagnosis and doubles as the first step of hydrodilatation treatment.

faqs.question: Will frozen shoulder go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases. Studies show that 90% of patients recover near-full function over 18 months to 3 years without surgery. However, up to 40% have some residual restriction at 3 years, and early treatment with corticosteroid injection significantly shortens the freezing phase and speeds return to function.

faqs.question: What is the most effective treatment for frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Corticosteroid injection into the glenohumeral joint is the most effective single early treatment, reducing pain and improving function at 6 weeks. Hydrodilatation (high-volume capsule distension under fluoroscopy) accelerates ROM recovery in the frozen phase. Physiotherapy is added after injection to consolidate gains.

faqs.question: How many cortisone injections can I have for frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Most guidelines recommend limiting glenohumeral corticosteroid injections to three over a 12-month period to avoid cumulative cartilage toxicity. A minimum 6-week interval between injections is recommended to assess response. Beyond three injections, hydrodilatation or surgical options should be considered.

faqs.question: Is frozen shoulder more common in people with diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. People with type 1 or type 2 diabetes have a 19–20% lifetime risk of frozen shoulder, compared with 2–5% in the general population. Diabetic frozen shoulder is more severe, more often bilateral (40–50%), and responds less well to corticosteroid injection. Optimising glycemic control improves treatment outcomes.

faqs.question: What exercises help frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Pendular (Codman) exercises — swinging the arm gently in circles while leaning forward — reduce pain in the freezing phase. External rotation stretch with the elbow at 90°, cross-body stretch, and doorframe stretch improve range of motion in the frozen and thawing phases. All should be performed after moist heat and within comfortable limits.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Surgery (manipulation under anaesthesia or arthroscopic capsular release) is considered when symptoms persist beyond 6 months of corticosteroid injection plus physiotherapy. Arthroscopic release is preferred for diabetic patients and after failed MUA. Both procedures achieve 85–95% good outcomes at 12 months.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from frozen shoulder surgery take?
faqs.answer: After arthroscopic capsular release, most patients regain full daily activity use within 4–8 weeks and return to sport or overhead work by 3–6 months with postoperative physiotherapy. Physiotherapy compliance in the first 6 weeks after surgery is critical to prevent re-adhesion.

faqs.question: Can frozen shoulder come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence in the same shoulder after surgical release is rare (under 5%). However, up to 17% of patients develop a second frozen shoulder in the opposite shoulder within 5 years, especially those with diabetes. Keeping the second shoulder gently mobile and optimising glycemic control are the best preventive measures.

faqs.question: What is the difference between frozen shoulder and a rotator cuff tear?
faqs.answer: Frozen shoulder restricts both active and passive movement in all directions with a firm capsular end-feel. A rotator cuff tear restricts active elevation but passive motion remains largely preserved. MRI or ultrasound of the shoulder shows intact capsule but torn tendon fibres in a rotator cuff tear.

faqs.question: Does frozen shoulder affect sleep?
faqs.answer: Yes. Night pain is one of the most disabling features of the freezing phase. Patients cannot lie on the affected side. Sleeping supine with a pillow under the arm in 30° of abduction, combined with pain medication before bed, helps reduce nocturnal symptoms. Sleep typically improves significantly once the frozen phase begins.

faqs.question: Can I drive with frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Driving is often painful or impossible during the freezing phase if the affected side is the gear-change or steering arm. As the frozen phase progresses, pain reduces and driving typically becomes manageable. Patients should not drive if pain or stiffness significantly impairs steering control.

symptomKeywords: shoulder stiff and painful at night
metaTitle: Frozen Shoulder: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis): progressive shoulder stiffness in 3 phases. Causes, corticosteroid injection, hydrodilatation and surgery reviewed.

sources.title: Buchbinder R, Green S, Youd JM, Johnston RV. 2003. Corticosteroid injections for shoulder pain. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2003;(1):CD004016.

Cho CH, Bae KC, Kim DH. 2019. Treatment strategy for frozen shoulder. Clin Orthop Surg 11(3):249-257.
Huang SW, Lin JW, Chang CK, et al. 2017. Hyperthyroidism is a risk factor for developing adhesive capsulitis. Sci Rep 7(1):4183.
American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons. Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis). OrthoInfo, 2023.
Neviaser RJ, Neviaser TJ. 1987. The frozen shoulder: diagnosis and management. Clin Orthop Relat Res 223:59-64.
